w:r.w:t: This document specifies the paramedic field handoff report feature for the EMR-to-Hospital app. It is a quick-fill checklist that a paramedic completes while transporting a patient to hospital — designed to be done in under 5 minutes, on a mobile phone, often one-handed and under stress.

The core principle is simple: paramedics observe, the EMR remembers. The paramedic only enters what they see at the scene and what they do en route. All prior patient data — allergies, medications, past history — is pre-filled from the EMR. The paramedic reviews, never re-types.

This feature is the missing link in India's emergency care chain. It bridges the EMR and the hospital intake system so that patient information arrives at the hospital at the same time as — or before — the patient.
In India, the handoff between paramedics and hospitals is entirely verbal and undocumented. When a patient arrives at a hospital:
The receiving doctor has no prior information about what happened at the scene.
The paramedic has no structured way to communicate vitals, interventions, or patient history.
Critical data — last medication dose, mechanism of injury, GCS trend — is lost or inconsistently conveyed.
If the patient is unconscious or alone, their history is completely unknown unless it exists in the EMR.
This feature solves the last-mile documentation gap by giving paramedics a fast, structured form that generates a report delivered to the hospital before or on arrival.
Paramedic or EMT in the field
Mobile phone (Android / iOS), one hand, moving vehicle
Intermittent — must work offline and sync when connected
High — form must be completable in under 5 minutes
At scene pickup or during transport to hospital
On arrival at hospital, or approximately 2 minutes before ETA

The checklist has 6 sequential sections. Each section has a time target. A horizontal progress bar at the top of the screen tracks completion. Each section has a numbered circle that fills green when done. Sections are displayed top to bottom — no tabs, no hidden panels. The paramedic scrolls through.
Dispatch time and arrival time are auto-stamped. No manual entry needed. The paramedic only needs to pick incident type and mechanism — 2 taps — to complete this section.
Section 2 — Vitals on Contact | Target: 60 seconds
Section marked done when at least 3 of the 6 vitals are entered. All 6 are preferred. A timestamp is auto-applied to the entire vitals entry when the first number is entered.
PEARL — Pupils Equal And Reactive to Light

The complaint field uses voice-to-text as the primary input mode. The paramedic speaks one sentence and the app transcribes it. Typing is the fallback. This is the only free-text field in the entire checklist.

If the incident type is Trauma or the complaint contains the word pain, the app optionally expands to show OPQRST sub-fields (Onset, Provocation, Quality, Radiation, Severity, Time). Not shown by default.
Tap checkbox, then: flow rate and mask type
Tap checkbox, then: site, gauge, fluid type
Tap checkbox, then: drug, dose, route, time
Tap checkbox, then: start time and duration
Tap checkbox, then: number of shocks

Each checkbox expands inline when tapped to show only the relevant sub-fields for that intervention. All intervention timestamps are auto-stamped when the checkbox is ticked. The paramedic can manually adjust time if the intervention happened before the app was opened.

Medication administered is the most critical sub-section. It must capture: drug name, dose in mg, route (IV / IM / oral / sublingual), and time given. This data must be readable by the receiving hospital immediately on report receipt.
Section 6 — Trend & Handoff Flag | Target: 15 seconds

Patient trend and receiving department are the two fields the hospital sees first — before they open the full report. These must be visually prominent in the handoff summary screen shown to the receiving nurse or doctor.

A horizontal progress bar sits fixed at the top of the checklist screen. It shows how many of 6 sections are marked done. Each section has a numbered circle indicator that turns green when the section minimum is met.
Section 1 done when — incident type is selected.
Section 2 done when — at least 3 vitals are entered.
Section 3 done when — AVPU is selected and pupils are selected.
Section 4 done when — complaint field has any text and onset is selected.
Section 5 done when — at least one checkbox is ticked, including 'no interventions needed'.
Section 6 done when — trend is selected and receiving department is selected.
The Submit button is always visible at the bottom of the screen. It can be tapped at any time. Partial reports are valid and clearly labelled as incomplete when received by the hospital.
On submit, the app does three things simultaneously:
Pushes the structured report to the receiving hospital's system via API.
Generates a one-sentence verbal handoff summary displayed on screen for the paramedic to read aloud at the hospital door.
Logs the report locally in case of no connectivity — syncs automatically when connection is restored.
[Name], [age], [sex] — [incident type] — [complaint], [onset onset]. Vitals: BP [x], SpO2 [x], GCS [x]. Interventions: [list]. Trend: [trend]. Alerting [department].

Rahul Sharma, 54, male — Cardiac — chest pain radiating to left arm, sudden onset. Vitals: BP 90/60, SpO2 91, GCS 15. Interventions: O2 at 6L non-rebreather mask, aspirin 325mg oral at 14:32. Trend: Deteriorating. Alerting Cath Lab.
This sentence is displayed in large font on the submission confirmation screen so the paramedic can read it hands-free while walking into the hospital.
The checklist must be fully functional with zero network connectivity. All data is written to local device storage as the paramedic fills the form.
Patient EMR data is cached on the device when the app is opened online — before the call begins.
If the patient is unregistered or a walk-in, the SAMPLE context section (allergies, medications, history) is left blank and the paramedic fills what they know manually.
Submission queues locally and retries automatically when connection is restored.
Reports submitted offline are flagged with an offline submission timestamp in the hospital system.
All fields in the checklist, their input type, whether they are required, and whether they are auto-filled from the EMR.
Every field must be fillable by tap. No field should require more than 2 taps to complete.
The only free-text field is the chief complaint. Everything else is tap-select or number input.
Voice-to-text must be available on the complaint field and the optional notes field.
The keyboard must not auto-open on section load. Number pads open only when a vitals field is tapped.
Tap targets must be a minimum of 44px height for gloved or stressed hands.
The form must not require scrolling inside a card. Cards expand to fit their content inline.
Patient name, age, blood group, allergies, current medications, and past medical history are pre-fetched from the EMR when the case is opened.
All timestamps — dispatch, scene arrival, vitals, interventions — are auto-stamped and user-editable. Never require manual time entry unless the paramedic explicitly corrects it.
The app must visually distinguish EMR-sourced fields from paramedic-entered fields with a small indicator badge.
No hard blocks — the form never prevents submission due to missing fields.
Soft warnings are shown for critical missing fields (BP, SpO2, AVPU, complaint) as a banner before the submit tap.
If fewer than 3 sections are complete, the report is flagged as Partial Handoff in the hospital system.
10. Out of Scope for This Feature
Full SOAP documentation — this is a handoff report, not a complete clinical note.
Billing, insurance, or Ayushman / PMJAY card fields — separate flow.
Photo or video capture from the scene — can be added in v2.
Hospital acceptance confirmation — separate handshake feature.
Real-time GPS tracking of the ambulance — infrastructure dependency, out of scope here.
Prepared for internal development — EMR-to-Hospital App | Paramedic Handoff Feature
